title: Iconic, Antique, Windsor, Gold Filled, Eye Glasses. Similar Spectacles Made Famous By Harry Potter and John Lennon.
description:
These iconic Windsor glasses are in very good condition. These style of glasses first emerged in the latter part of the 19th century and were generally popular through the first part of the 20th century. They are known by many name which include granny glasses, tea glasses, John Lennon Glasses and more recently Harry Potter Glasses. 
I believe these glasses were manufactured in the first part of the 20th century. They are stamped with Leroy on the bridge along with what looks like a gold stamp. (see photo). Leroy Opticians described themselves as the premiere opticians in Paris. This boast was advertised in a famous poster that can be still bought.
Windsor glasses are typified by a thin metal frame that has a saddle which joined the circular frames and riding bow temples. (which are the arms with bendy ends that go around the ear.) In  later years Windsors'  had nose pads and omitted the bendy arms.

description:

title: A Striking  Large, White, 19th Century, French, Antique, Wood, Hand Carved, Decorative, Antique, French, Pediment, Fronton for Chateau Chic
description:
This is a striking large white chalk painted wooden antique  19th century French pediment.

It is a beautiful decorative item, these used to be found in both the French town and country properties, usually adorned on a wardrobe or armoire. Although often found on wardrobes they could also be seen on bedheads or door frames.

The pediment or “frontons” were always separate pieces of wood and some of these got removed when fashions changed. Now with the return of characters and the quality they are becoming increasingly sought after. We have the privilege of owning a house in France that was previously owned by a carpenter which made us fall in love with these wonderful pieces all over again.

They look incredible either on bedroom furniture or above a door frame. These are a clever way to add some traditional French chateau charm to your home and to have a completely unique handcrafted part of history incorporated beautifully into your room.

This piece has been has been painted with chalk paint

Sourced locally in the south of France .

title: Rare Set Of 4  Antique, French, Heavy, Green Glass, Beer, Lemonade, Bottles, Sold Separately, Early 20th Century, Olonzac, South Of France
description:
These are a rare  set of six French beer and lemonade bottles from the beginning of the 20th century.  None of the six bottles have any chips on the glass. There is very slight grazing of the glass where the porcelain stopper has bounced on 2 of them, this can hardly be seen. The porcelain stoppers are very slightly worn, with only one of them having a  very small sliver missing ( see photo 3). All the stoppers have clearly written on them SARDA. OLONZAC. The stopper wire has slight signs of rust, but are clean. These bottles are hand blown in a closed mold. You can see the seam line stops below the finish (lip), so it looks like it has an applied tooled finish. The glass is a lovely olive green and has embossed on them, "Bieres & Limonades, SARDA OLONZAC. The glass has attractive uneven patterns on it and some air bubbles , from when it is hand blown into the mold, also thick uneven bases on the inside (see photo second from last). One of the bottles has a different font and C.Sarda embossed. It looks like a slightly later design, possibly the son. As far as I know they started their business in 1906 and there are still members of the same family still living in the village, which is in the Herault and has a population  of about 1600 people.

I am selling these bottles individually.

title: Rare, Antique, 19th Century, Early, French, Brasserie, Beer Bottle, Hand Blown, Thick, Green Glass, Philippe Lauth, Carcassonne
description:
This is a rare, 19th century, early French Brasserie, green glass, beer bottle. It is hand blow into a mold with a one part blob finish that is  wrapped around the cracked-off/sheared neck. You can see bubbles in the finish which also has a wavy horizontal line, which is probably caused by the laying-on tool. On the body of the bottle there is a surface blemish ( see photos) and bubbles which are vertically stretched, caused when the bottle is pulled upwards. There are also very faint horizontal striations caused by the bottle being turned while molten. It has a lovely thick uneven base with several small bubbles. 
 It is in excellent condition with only the small glass blemish on the body. It has acid etched on the body "Brasserie Philippe Lauth, Carcassonne".
 Philippe Lauth's breweries were in Sete and Carcassonne. He took over the running of the breweries in 1872 from his father Frederic. This is one of the first two breweries to open in Carcassonne They finally closed in 1939.

description:
A  lovely wooden antique French pediment. The  carving can be seen as  an owl and is symbolic of wisdom . These traditional pieces are national treasures and finding one as wonderful as this is rare.

It is a beautiful decorative item, these used to be found in both the French town and country properties, usually adorned on a wardrobe or armoire. Although often found on wardrobes they could also be seen on bedheads or door frames.

The pediment or “frontons” were always separate pieces of wood and some of these got removed when fashions changed. Now with the return of characters and the quality they are becoming increasingly sought after. We have the privilege of owning a house in France that was previously owned by a carpenter which made us fall in love with these wonderful pieces all over again.

They look incredible either on bedroom furniture or above a door frame. These are a clever way to add some traditional French chateau charm to your home and to have a completely unique handcrafted part of history incorporated beautifully into your room.

This piece has been cleaned and the wood treated with all-natural and traditional oils so it is ready to be placed where ever you wish. Obviously, as a handcrafted piece of this age it does have a few marks, chips(can be seen in the photos), but remarkably few for its age. title: Antique 19th Century Silver Metal Pince Nez with Original Leather Case
description:
Step back in time with this exquisite pair of 19th-century silver metal pince nez, a true testament to the craftsmanship and style of a bygone era. These vintage eyeglasses are not only a functional piece of history but also a collector's dream, coming complete with their original leather case, needs a small stitching repair on one side of the case.  
Pince nez, French for 'pinch nose,' were a popular style of spectacles in the 19th and early 20th centuries, favoured for their elegant design and the convenience of not having temples (arms) to rest on the ears. Instead, they are designed to be held in place by pinching the bridge of the nose, making them a unique and intriguing accessory.  
This particular pair features oval lenses set in delicate silver metal frames, showcasing the fine artistry of the period. The frames are in good condition, with no significant damage or wear, preserving their antique charm. The lenses are clear and intact, ready to be fitted with your prescription or left as is for display purposes.  
Adding to the allure of this set is the original leather case, which has been well-preserved over the years. The case not only protects the pince nez but also serves as a historical artifact in its own right, offering a glimpse into the past.  
Whether you are a collector of antique eyewear, a history enthusiast, or someone who appreciates vintage fashion, these pince nez are sure to captivate your interest. They make for a stunning display piece, a conversation starter, or even a unique addition to a costume or theatrical production. 
 
Item Details:  
Material: Silver metal  

Era: 19th century  

Condition: Good (frames, lenses, and case)  

Lens Shape: Oval 

Frame Features: No temples, nose-gripping bridge, loop for chain, Nose cutters  

Included: Original leather case

These pince nez are not only a functional piece of eyewear but also a window into the past, reflecting the fashion and technological advancements of their time. The silver metal frames exude a sense of elegance and sophistication, while the round lenses offer a classic look that never goes out of style.  
The absence of temples makes these glasses particularly interesting, as they rely on a spring mechanism to stay securely on the nose. This design was especially popular among the upper classes and intellectuals of the 19th century, including notable figures such as Theodore Roosevelt and Anton Chekhov.  
Owning a pair of authentic 19th-century pince nez is like holding a piece of history in your hands. Whether displayed in a shadow box, used as part of a historical reenactment, or simply admired for their beauty, these glasses are sure to be a cherished addition to any collection.

title: Rare,Antique, French, Small, Green Glass, Lemonade,Beer, Bottle, Late 19th Century,With Early Lightening Closure, Olonzac, South Of France
description:
This is an example of an early lemonade or beer bottle,in fine condition. This  French lemonade/beer bottle is  from the late 19th century or the beginning of the 20th century. No chips on the glass. ( see photos). The stopper has clearly written on it MARTY AZILE. The lightening closure on the bottle is an unusual early model where the wire is tied around the bottle, these were commonly used between 1890-1915. The stopper wire has slight signs of rust, but are clean.  These bottles are hand blown in a closed mold. You can see the seam line stops below the finish (lip), so it looks like it has an applied tooled finish.The glass is green and has an attractive uneven patterns on it, from when it is hand blown into the mold, also an uneven base on the inside. There is white deposits on the inside of the bottle. (see photo ).This one has no raised lettering on the outside but it is acid etched with the words. "OLANZAC OSCAR ROUAYROUX (HERAULT) BIERES, BOISSONS GAZEUSES". The village Olonzac, which is in the Herault has a population of about 1600 people.

title: Rare, Exceptional, Antique, French, Small, Clear, Lemonade, Bottle, Early 20th Century, Clement Sarda, Olonzac, South Of France
description:
This is a exceptional condition French lemonade bottle from the beginning of the 20th century.  No chips on the glass or porcelain stopper. It also has a complete rubber gasket ( see photos). The stopper has clearly written on it SARDA. OLONZAC. The stopper wire has slight signs of rust, but are clean.  It has a lightning-type swing closures which were most popular on beer and many soda bottles from the 1880s into the 1920s. These bottles are hand blown in a closed mold. You can see the seam line stops below the finish (lip), so it looks like it has an applied tooled finish. The glass is a lovely clear glass with a greenish tint and has embossed on them, CLEMENT SARDA, Limonades, OLONZAC. The glass has attractive uneven patterns on it, from when it is hand blown into the mold, also thick uneven bases on the inside (see photo ). . As far as I know they started their business in 1906 and there are still members of the same family still living in the village, which is in the Herault and has a population  of about 1600 people.

title: Lovely, Antique, French, Handblown, Green Glass Bottle, With Blob Seal on front saying "Founded in 1807", Aperitif, Brun-Perod distillery
description:
This is lovely antique green glass aperitif bottle. It is made by the Brun-Perod distillery, who were famous for inventing the liqueur China-China in 1812, which I believe was in this bottle. The distillery was in Voiron, France. It was a digestive liquor based on bark of bitter oranges, aromatic plants and exotic fruits.The story of its creation is quite funny. This I got from a French blog.  "In the absence of his wife, Brun, who was entertaining a few friends, took some liberties with his cook, called China, who prepared the meal. " let's go! Monsieurs, ne China pas, ne China pas” and the master continued to prattle: “Si, si China-China”… So much so that the orange compote burned and turned a caramel black. Brun saved the situation by pouring  various alcohols into the saucepan and served his guests his latest creation: a black chartreuse that his friends jokingly called “China-China” this beverage was a big success…"

title: Antique French Pince Nez Glasses, Late 18th to Mid-19th Century
description:
Step into the past with these exquisite antique French Pince Nez glasses, a rare treasure from the late 18th to mid-19th century. Crafted during an era of elegance and innovation, these glasses feature round lenses framed in rich brown-marbled finish, a material beloved for its sophisticated, mottled brown pattern. The frames are connected by a delicate metal bridge with a spring-loaded mechanism, allowing the glasses to perch securely on the nose without earpieces—a hallmark of Pince Nez design. A small metal loop with a bead, likely for attaching a chain or cord, adds both practicality and charm. These glasses are more than eyewear; they are a tangible piece of history, offering a glimpse into the refined tastes and craftsmanship of French artisans from a bygone era.  
Pince Nez glasses, meaning "pinch nose" in French, first emerged in the 18th century as a stylish alternative to traditional spectacles. They quickly became a favorite among French aristocrats, intellectuals, and professionals, who valued their discreet elegance and ease of use. Unlike modern glasses, Pince Nez relied on a gentle clamping mechanism to stay in place, making them both functional and fashionable. This pair, with its timeless design, embodies the aesthetic of the period—a time when eyewear was as much a statement of style as it was a tool for vision.  
Each round lens, remains clear and in excellent condition, free from significant scratches or cracks, a testament to their careful preservation. The metal components, including the bridge and loops, show a natural tarnish, a sign of their age and authenticity. This gentle patina enhances their vintage appeal, adding character without compromising their integrity. The brown-marbled finish frames are intact, their intricate pattern still vibrant, making these glasses as beautiful as they are historical.  
Displayed against a richly textured fabric, these glasses evoke the elegance of the 18th and 19th centuries, a period marked by the transition from Rococo extravagance to Neoclassical restraint in French design. Their historical significance and unique charm make them a perfect addition to any collection of antique eyewear or vintage decor. Whether you’re a collector, a history enthusiast, or someone seeking a distinctive accessory for steampunk fashion or reenactments, these Pince Nez glasses are a rare find that bridges the past and present with grace.  
In terms of condition, these glasses are remarkably well-preserved for their age. The lenses retain their clarity, offering a glimpse into the optical technology of the time, while the frames show only minor signs of wear. The tarnishing on the metal components adds to their authenticity, making them ideal for display in a curio cabinet or as part of a vintage-themed decor.

The 18th and 19th centuries in France were marked by political upheaval, artistic innovation, and rapid industrialization, which led to a rise in the demand for more refined personal accessories like eyeglasses. The design of these spectacles reflects the enduring charm of French design during the Napoleonic and post-Revolutionary eras. title: Three, Iconic, Antique, Early 20th Century, Antesite, Bottles, Sold individually, They were used for a Famous Concentrated, Liquorice Drink.
description:
Three iconic good condition antique French, Antesite, Liquorice, cordial, drink bottles for sale. This drink has an interesting history behind it. It was invented in 1898 with the aim of reducing alcoholism among the working population, especially the French railway workers, who added wine or anise to their water. The name comes from the Latin, ante-sitis,  meaning against thirst.  It is still being made today. I believe these bottles were manufactured in the first part of the 20th century.

These three bottles are being sold for 15 euros each .

title: Rare Set Of 8 Brasserie Bottles, Sold Separately, Great Condition, Antique, French, Beer Bottles, Early 20th Cent, Fritz Lauer, Carcassonne
description:
This are a rare set of eight French antique brasserie beer bottles from the beginning of the 20th century. The stopper wire are clean.  None of the bottles have have any chips to the glass except one that has a very small nibbles at the lip. These bottles are hand blown in a closed mold. You can see the seam line stops below the finish (lip), so it looks like it has an applied tooled finish. The colour of the glass is a lovely olive green and brown and has embossed on them, "BRASSERIE FRITZ LAUER CARCASSONNE" around an eight pointed star that contain an overflowing beer mug. The glass has attractive uneven patterns on it and some air bubbles , from when it is hand blown into the mold, also thick uneven bases on the inside (see photos). Six of the bottles have the beer mug logo on the stoppers and two stoppers are without the logo. The famous Carcassonne Brasserie opened in 1914 and closed forever in 1951.

I am selling these bottles individually.

title: Vintage Silver Metal Oval Glasses with Saddle Bridge & Riding Bow Temples – Late 19th to Early 20th Century
description:
Step back in time and add a touch of vintage French elegance to your collection with these stunning silver metal oval frame glasses. Dating back to the late 19th to early 20th century, these glasses are a true reflection of the craftsmanship and style of the era. With a unique saddle bridge and riding bow temples, they’re as functional as they are fashionable, offering a glimpse into the sophisticated eyewear trends of the past.
The saddle bridge is one of the standout features of these glasses, offering both comfort and style. Instead of the modern nose pads, the saddle bridge gently rests on the nose, providing a secure and comfortable fit. This design was a hallmark of vintage eyewear from the late 1800s and early 1900s, and it remains a charming feature for lovers of antique glasses.
The riding bow temples, another distinctive design element, curve gently behind the ears, giving the glasses an elegant, streamlined appearance. Unlike the more modern, straight temples, these curved arms were designed to offer a secure fit and a more stylish profile. It's details like this that make these vintage glasses so special and a perfect piece of history to wear or display.
The silver metal frame itself is in good condition, with some natural signs of age, which only add to the charm and authenticity of the piece. The oval shape of the lenses is classic and timeless, with just enough curvature to provide a unique look. The lenses are also in great shape, free of major scratches or cracks, making these glasses not only a collector’s item but also a functional accessory.
These vintage glasses are a perfect example of the eyewear style that was beloved in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Glasses of this era were crafted with a focus on both practicality and style, and the combination of the saddle bridge, oval frame, and riding bow temples speaks to the elegance of the time. This is the type of eyewear worn by intellectuals, fashion-forward individuals, and those who appreciated well-made accessories.
Whether you’re a collector looking to add a rare piece to your vintage eyewear collection or someone who simply loves to wear antique, one-of-a-kind accessories, these French glasses are a perfect choice. They’re ideal for pairing with a vintage-inspired outfit or for those who appreciate the finer details in fashion history.
So, why not bring home a piece of French history? These vintage silver metal glasses are sure to make a statement wherever you go, offering both style and a touch of nostalgia that only antique eyewear can provide.

title: Vintage 19th Century Silver Sunglasses with Light Blue Tint – French Oval Lenses, Saddlebridge Nose Support
description:
Step into the world of 19th-century elegance with these stunning silver metal sunglasses, straight from France! With their iconic oval lenses and subtle light blue tint, these glasses are a unique, vintage treasure that brings both history and style into your wardrobe. Whether you’re a collector or just someone with a flair for vintage accessories, these sunglasses are sure to make a statement.
Crafted from silver metal, these glasses feature a saddle bridge nose support, a design choice that ensures a comfortable and secure fit on your face. Unlike modern glasses, this style has no traditional ear hooks— instead, the saddle bridge rests gently on the nose, offering a vintage feel with a functional twist. The straight arms extend outward for a more minimalist look that was highly popular in the late 1800s and early 1900s.
One of the standout features of these sunglasses is their oval lenses, which are in good condition for their age. The soft light blue tint is a classic 19th-century touch, providing just the right amount of sun protection while maintaining a delicate, ethereal look. Though the frames show a few rust marks—a natural result of age—these imperfections only add to the character and charm of this rare find. They serve as a testament to their authenticity and history, giving them a truly vintage, worn-in look.
If you’re wondering about the history of these glasses, it’s important to know that eyewear in the late 19th and early 20th centuries was both a necessity and a statement of style. The design of glasses from this era was largely focused on practicality, but fashion-conscious individuals made sure their eyewear reflected their taste. Back then, glasses often featured intricate detailing, minimalist forms, and creative nose supports—like the saddlebridge—rather than the modern arms we know today. This made glasses not just functional, but a true reflection of the person wearing them.
The early 20th century saw the rise of various eyewear trends, including tinted lenses, which were believed to protect the eyes from the harsh sun, much like modern sunglasses. While these pieces were worn for protection, they also symbolized the elite and fashionable class. Owning a pair of these sunglasses is like holding a small piece of history that was once worn by the chic and fashionable of their time.
These vintage sunglasses are perfect for anyone with a love of antique eyewear or for those seeking something unique to complete their retro-inspired look. Whether you’re adding to a collection, dressing up for a themed event, or simply want to wear a piece of history, these French silver sunglasses are the perfect choice.

title: Vintage 19th Century Silver Metal, Pince Nez, Nose Cutters, Sunglasses with Green Tint - French Charm
description:
Step back in time and add a unique piece of history to your collection with these stunning 19th-century silver metal pince nez sunglasses. These charming pieces of eyewear aren't just functional—they're an embodiment of vintage French elegance! Designed with the original nose clip mechanism, offering both style and practicality.
This pair of pince nez is crafted from silver metal and features oval lenses with a subtle green tint that provides a soft, yet stylish look. Perfect for those who appreciate vintage accessories with a story to tell, this pair is in good condition for its age, making it a perfect find for collectors and fashion enthusiasts alike.
A little history lesson for you: the pince nez, French for "nose pinch," was a popular style of eyeglasses in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. The name comes from the unique way the glasses stay on your face—without arms or earpieces, they simply "pinch" the nose. This practical design made them incredibly popular in the Victorian era, worn by fashionable men and women across Europe, including France, where they became synonymous with sophisticated style.
The green tint of these lenses gives them an added vintage flair while offering a touch of sun protection. Although these sunglasses are functional for everyday use, their true beauty lies in their antique charm, making them a conversation starter wherever you go. Whether you’re a collector of rare vintage pieces or someone who loves to wear distinctive eyewear, these pince nez are sure to catch the eye of anyone who appreciates craftsmanship.
The nose clip design is still in excellent working order, and the oval lenses are free from any major scratches or imperfections, making them a fantastic addition to your vintage eyewear collection. You’ll love how this piece of history feels and looks on your face, adding that perfect touch of retro sophistication to any outfit.
Whether you're adding to a collection, looking for something unique for cosplay, or simply want to wear a functional yet vintage accessory, these French pince nez sunglasses are a must-have. They’re a true reflection of 19th-century eyewear innovation and French style.

title: Set of 4 French vintage, Obernai dessert plates, bread plates, side plates by Henri Loux
description:
Set of 4 vintage dessert plates or bread plates, side plates called Obernai that are signed by H. LOUX.

These plates are from the pottery in Sarreguemines, France. The are in very good condition and have the Rohan design, which is indicated by the three bouquets. As opposed to the Rouen design which has four.

After the death of Henri Loux in 1907, the service took the name Obernai. In Obernai, an innkeeper wanted to complete the Loux service but as Henri Loux having died, the service could no longer be called Loux and this is how he obtained the name Obernai from 1907 to the present day.

The Alsatian Obernai table service has 56 different patterns 52 of these are designed by Henri Loux. These feature are variety of pastoral scenes from Alsace with people wearing traditional clothing from the area.

title: Vintage, pair of 1960s, Mid-Century, Modern, Pierre Guariche Style, French, Space Age, Plastic, Perspex, Pivoting, Cone Wall Lights, Sconces
description:
These stunning mid century French modern wall lights were designed in the 1960s, what is interesting about them is that they share the same design motifs as Pierre Gauriche's workshop  from the 1950's. They are mounted on  black curvy plastic freeform wall plates that resembles  a painter's palette and the red stem downwards that is reminiscent of a brush. with a fully orienting brass joint and magnificent translucent corrugated plexiglass cone shades. the wall plates of these sconces are exactly the same as those attributed to Gariche's workshop that operated in the mid 1950's. It is possible that the designs from the 1950's was still being manufactured  by a factory in France in the 1960's.As they are not marked it would be impossible to 100% attribute them directly to Paul Gauriche's Atelier.

Jean Pierre Guariche (1926-1995) was a French designer . He had a design office with two other designers, Michel Mortier (1925-2015) and Joseph-André Motte (1925-2013) that was called A.R.P. which stood for L’ Atelier de Recherche Plastique (Plastic Research Workshop). This Atelier existed   for only three years (1954-1957) ; it focused on design with plastic, as at the time it was seen as very modern. 

Superbly French but also with quintessential Mid Century atomic style, from an era when colour and futuristic design were seen  as the epitome of sophistication. They are very eye catching and will look stunning in any room.

They fix to the wall with a black plastic bracket, both lamps have them.

The ideal gift for anyone with a love of mid-century modern style and a fantastic decorators piece. Ideal for any room and they could be the perfect prop for a 1950s/60s period film.

As you can see they are currently in working order though the wiring is very old and it may be advisable to rewire them.  If you have any concerns about safety you should get them professionally tested after fitting.

title: Vintage, 19th Century, Pince Nez, Nose Cutters, Sunglasses – French Steel Frame with Green Tint Lenses
description:
Step back in time and add a touch of vintage sophistication to your eyewear collection with these exquisite 19th-century French pince-nez, nose cutters, sunglasses. This antique pair, with their unique green-tinted lenses, showcases the elegance and ingenuity of eyewear from a bygone era. Carefully crafted with a steel sprung frame, they offer both durability and comfort, making them a stunning historical piece that’s as functional as it is fashionable.

Key Features:
    • Authentic French Craftsmanship: These pince-nez were crafted in France during the 19th century, offering a true piece of history for collectors or those who appreciate antique accessories. 
    • Green-Tinted Lenses: The lenses are gently tinted in a soothing green hue, which was commonly used in the 19th century to reduce glare while providing optimal vision in bright conditions. This vintage tint still serves its purpose while adding an intriguing old-world charm. 
    • Steel Sprung Frame: The frame is made of high-quality steel, designed to last while maintaining flexibility and comfort. The sprung design ensures a secure fit on the nose without the need for ear temples, which was a hallmark of the pince-nez design. 
    • Nose Pads in Good Condition: Despite their age, the nose pads remain in excellent condition, offering a comfortable and secure fit for the wearer. The pads help balance the frame’s weight while resting comfortably on the nose. 
    • Historical Appeal: Pince-nez eyewear was a popular style in the late 19th century and into the early 20th century. They were favored by both men and women and were often worn as a sign of intellect and sophistication. Famous historical figures, including writers, artists, and politicians, often sported pince-nez as their go-to accessory. 
A Bit of History:
The pince-nez, French for "pinch nose," is a style of eyewear that was held in place by pinching the nose rather than being supported by ear temples. This unique design made pince-nez glasses more compact and portable than traditional spectacles. They were most popular from the mid-1800s through the early 1900s, after which more modern eyeglass styles began to dominate. Pince-nez eyewear has since become a symbol of vintage fashion and historical elegance.
Whether you’re a collector of antique eyewear or someone looking for a distinctive accessory, these 19th-century French pince-nez nose cutters are a must-have. Their timeless design and functional beauty make them a versatile piece, perfect for display or for use in your everyday life. The green tint offers just the right amount of vintage flair while providing a practical level of comfort and eye protection.

title: Antique Large Wooden Pilgrimage Rosary, wall decoration - Notre Dame de Lourdes Souvenir 19th century
description:

 The beads and the cross are made of wood (probably olive wood), as is the heart-shaped pendant in the middle. The beads are hand carved in a rose setting and vary slightly in size and design. It is very tactile. This rosary dates from around 1900 and is a souvenir from Lourdes. It is hand carved, probably in the monastery in Lourdes. It is in good condition, but has age and use  wear as would be expected. It is possible that there was a brass Jesus originally on it. This does not detract from the overall attraction of the rosary. This could be used as a wall decoration or broken up to use the beads for another project.
